Summary: Adjusted the Health Records feature to be cache-first and preserve visible cached content during background refreshes. The shared data screen now accepts repository injection for easier testing, keeps cached records visible when the network returns an empty list, and still falls back to loading/error states only for true first-load/no-cache cases. The shared template no longer shows a loading overlay once entries are already visible, which keeps the UI smooth and consistent with the Personal Records behavior.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Preserve cached Health Records content when the refresh response is empty so the screen does not flash from populated content to empty content.
2. [finished] Remove the background loading overlay from the Health Records template because cached content is the primary fast path and the extra indicator was not needed for already-visible data.
3. [finished] Use repository injection in the HealthRecordDataScreen instead of mocking secure storage directly, keeping the screen testable without widening unrelated responsibilities.

Summary: Fixed the stuck 'session is refreshing' auth loop by removing the foreground timer branch that was incorrectly setting reauthentication instead of performing a silent refresh. The auth session now stays in refresh mode and no longer traps users on the MPIN lock screen or login flow just because the refresh timer fired.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept the refresh timer silent and non-destructive so it can refresh automatically without forcing a visible reauth state loop.
2. [finished] Retained the reauthentication flag for real auth failures, but no longer let the timer itself manufacture that state in the foreground.
3. [finished] Did not change the MPIN verify backend flow because the immediate bug was in the frontend refresh timer behavior.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — Core session lifecycle logic was fixed so the timer no longer forces reauthentication in the foreground and instead performs a silent refresh. | Notes: This is the direct fix for the stuck 'session is refreshing' loop.
2. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_unlock_screen.dart — Unlock flow was reviewed to confirm it still uses the session state correctly and only shows refresh messaging when the session truly needs it. | Notes: No changes were required in this step.
3. [inspected] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — App-level route redirection was reviewed to make sure the reauth logic still points to MPIN lock only when appropriate. | Notes: No changes were required in this step.

Summary: Fixed the MPIN unlock loop by clearing the reauthentication flag after a successful MPIN verification. The prior behavior preserved the session but left the app marked for reauthentication, which could cause the global auth listener to keep the user stuck on the MPIN screen instead of returning them to the app.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Use MPIN unlock success to clear the reauthentication marker because the unlock itself is the intended recovery path after a transient expired-session lock.
2. [finished] Keep the session-preservation behavior for refresh failures unchanged, because the user’s complaint was being trapped in MPIN, not losing session storage.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_unlock_screen.dart — This is the direct unlock path; it now clears reauthentication state after successful verification so navigation can continue normally. | Notes: Single-line behavioral fix.
2. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — The session state machine was reviewed to confirm that preserve-on-refresh-failure behavior remains in place and that reauth flags are the relevant blocker. | Notes: No further edits needed in this pass.
3. [inspected] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — The global auth listener was reviewed because it is what can re-route the app back into lock or login based on auth flags. | Notes: Behavior unchanged for now.

Summary: Fixed the auth logout-on-expiry behavior by preserving the persisted session during refresh failure and treating it as a reauthentication/lock state instead of clearing storage immediately. The app can now continue to route to MPIN lock without discarding the current session data unless the user explicitly signs out or there is truly no stored session.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Preserved expired-but-still-stored sessions on refresh failure to keep the user in a locked state instead of forcing a logout feel, even if the backend refresh token cannot be renewed immediately.
2. [finished] Kept hard logout behavior only for explicit sign-out paths and truly missing persisted sessions, because that is the safest place to clear state.
3. [finished] Retained the existing MPIN backend verification flow rather than converting MPIN into a fully local unlock secret store.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — This is the core auth-session state machine; it now preserves stored sessions during refresh failure and marks the user for reauthentication instead of clearing them. | Notes: This is the key fix for the logout-on-expiry bug.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — The app-level route observer handles the reauthentication lock routing and must remain aligned with the new preserved-session behavior. | Notes: It continues to route to MPIN lock when appropriate.
3. [inspected] frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart — Startup routing was reviewed to ensure expired authenticated sessions still land in MPIN unlock rather than hard login when possible. | Notes: No additional change was required here for this fix.
4. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_unlock_screen.dart — Unlock flow was checked to make sure it still uses the preserved session and does not force a logout unnecessarily. | Notes: No change was required in this step.
5. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_api_client.dart — 401/403 handling was reviewed because it triggers reauthentication state transitions and can influence whether the app feels logged out. | Notes: This remains a possible follow-up area if the issue persists.

Summary: Adjusted the app to stop silently keeping users in the foreground forever and instead trigger a reauthentication flow when the session ages out or an authenticated API call returns 401/403. The app shell now listens for an explicit AuthSession reauth-required flag, routes to MPIN unlock when MPIN is enabled, and falls back to login when needed. AuthSession now exposes and clears this flag on successful login/storage restore, foreground expiry, and hard refresh failure. Several API clients now signal reauthentication on unauthorized responses, and manual sign-out paths clear the flag before wiping session state to avoid redirect loops.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept the redirect decision centralized in the app shell, while using the domain/session layer only to signal that reauthentication is required.
2. [finished] Used MPIN unlock as the preferred reauthentication destination when MPIN is enabled and the user still has a valid session, so the app can recover without forcing a full password login.
3. [finished] Preserved login fallback for cases where the session is gone or MPIN is unavailable, rather than forcing a broken unlock flow.

Summary: Extended the Medical History experience to show richer patient-facing Condition and Procedure details from FHIR sync payloads. The backend mapper now stores more clinical context in detailsJson for medical history records, the frontend medical history screen copy/filtering was improved, and the task was completed and moved to Done. No SQL schema change was required because the existing details_json column already supports the richer fields.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the medical history schema stable and use the existing details_json column instead of adding new table columns, because the new patient-facing fields are display-oriented and do not require a schema rewrite.
2. [finished] Render the richer medical history information through the existing generic record UI so the feature improves without creating a special-case screen branch for Condition or Procedure records.
3. [finished] Normalize filter comparisons in the shared health-records template so the updated medical history filter labels remain usable even when stored values include hyphens or mixed casing.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Expanded Condition and Procedure mapping to persist richer patient-facing details and added date formatting plus performer display improvements. | Notes: No database shape change was introduced; the richer details flow through detailsJson.

[modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.spec.ts — Added regression assertions to confirm the new medical-history fields are emitted for Condition and Procedure resources. | Notes: Tests cover current status, verification status, diagnosis/performed dates, notes, outcome, and follow-up.

Summary: Implemented transaction-based correlation for sync receive-results while keeping the simulation path intact. The integration prepare flow now captures the gateway transaction id for each requested resource and conditionally stores a new fhir_sync_transactions row when the frontend provides the requester profile id via x-user-id. The FHIR sync service now resolves receive-results by transactionId first, falling back to identifier-based patient resolution when no transaction mapping exists, which fixes the Condition callback failure caused by missing patient inference. Frontend prepare requests now include x-user-id, and the app E2E plus backend integration/FHIR sync tests pass after the fix.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep transaction persistence optional at the service boundary so simulation can continue to reuse prepareSyncRequest without requiring a requester profile id.
2. [finished] Use a dedicated fhir_sync_transactions table instead of overloading patient identifiers or result payload fields for callback correlation.
3. [finished] Leave PH Core validation in place and change only the correlation step, so the callback fix does not weaken resource validation.

Summary: Fixed a live gateway 502/400 receive-results rejection for Condition resources. The gateway sent a Condition in a Bundle with meta.profile set to the base FHIR profile http://hl7.org/fhir/StructureDefinition/Condition, while WAH4P backend required the PH Core suffix /phcore/StructureDefinition/ph-core-condition. The fix added a narrow Condition-only compatible profile allowance and improved Condition recordedAt mapping to use recordedDate/onsetDateTime/abatementDateTime/performedDateTime before period fallbacks. Parser/service/E2E tests passed.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept profile compatibility narrow to Condition instead of broadly accepting generic FHIR profiles for every clinical resource type, preserving stricter validation elsewhere.
2. [finished] Added a parser regression test using the generic base FHIR Condition profile so future changes do not reintroduce the gateway receive-results rejection.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.profile.ts — Added Condition-only compatible profile support for the generic base FHIR Condition profile used by the gateway payload. | Notes: PH Core suffix validation remains the primary path for all supported resources.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Improved Condition recordedAt derivation so incoming Condition payloads with recordedDate or onsetDateTime map to the intended health-record date. | Notes: This also benefits PH Core Condition fixture mapping.
3. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.spec.ts — Added regression coverage proving generic base FHIR Condition profiles are accepted and mapped with the intended recordedAt date. | Notes: Existing missing-profile rejection test remains unchanged.

Summary: Added one more inspection after the FHIR live-payload investigation: FhirSyncRepository.findProfileIdByIdentifiers returns null when the Condition-only callback has no extractable identifiers. Therefore, after redeploying the array/profile fixes, the current audit payload may pass PH Core profile validation but then fail with Unable to match the received result to a patient profile unless the callback contains a Patient resource, subject.identifier, another extractable identifier, or a transaction/profile mapping is added.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Do not treat the possible follow-up patient matching error as the same PH Core profile validation bug; it is a separate correlation problem.
2. [ongoing] The clean production fix for Condition-only receive-results correlation should use explicit identifiers in the resource/callback or a stored transaction-to-profile mapping, not weakened PH Core validation.

The backend receive-results handler previously only accepted a FHIR Bundle, but the live gateway audit log showed the gateway forwarding SUCCESS data as a raw array of resources for the Condition callback. Regression tests now cover both the standard Bundle path and the array payload path, while the existing PH Core profile validation remains in place for Condition and other clinical resources.

Summary: Adjusted frontend auth UX so expired but refreshable sessions route into the MPIN unlock flow instead of logging the user out. Session refresh now distinguishes between transient refresh failures and hard refresh-token failures, preserving locked state for the former and forcing login only for invalid/expired refresh tokens.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept the solution in the frontend auth/session boundary because the problem was session lifecycle handling and the backend already supports refresh-based recovery.
2. [finished] Used MPIN unlock as the post-expiry gate only when the session remains refreshable; hard refresh failures still fall back to login to avoid trapping users in a dead-end lock screen.
3. [finished] Preserved the existing refresh-before-expiry behavior so users should usually never see the token-expired state during normal use.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — Centralized refresh/session expiry detection and preserved expired-but-refreshable sessions so the app can route them into the MPIN lock flow. | Notes: Added savedAt tracking, isAccessTokenExpired, and refresh-failure handling that differentiates transient and hard failures.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart — Startup route selection now sends expired authenticated sessions to MPIN unlock instead of login when MPIN is available. | Notes: This is the main entrypoint change for the new lock-state UX.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — App resume handling now checks for expired sessions and can push the MPIN unlock route directly. | Notes: This complements startup routing and keeps the UX consistent after backgrounding.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/presentation/screens/mpin_unlock_screen.dart — The unlock screen now refreshes the session before MPIN verification, so an expired access token can be renewed as part of the unlock flow. | Notes: This keeps MPIN unlock functional after access-token expiry.
5. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_local_store.dart — The persisted session model already gained expiry helpers needed by the refresh/lock flow and continues to back the new logic. | Notes: Existing helper coverage remains in the auth local store test file.
6. [created] frontend/test/features/auth/data/auth_local_store_test.dart — Regression coverage for session expiry helpers remains relevant to the new lock-before-login behavior. | Notes: The test still validates expiry timing logic.

content:
Summary: Improved frontend auth-session UX so access tokens refresh proactively before expiry and sessions are not dropped immediately on transient refresh failures. Also refreshed session state on app resume before MPIN/security checks, reducing surprise logouts while keeping auth boundaries intact.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept the fix in the frontend auth/session layer rather than changing backend token lifetimes, because the user-facing issue was session churn and the refresh workflow already exists server-side.
2. [finished] Used a proactive refresh window plus a short retry delay for transient failures so users are less likely to hit an expired token during normal use.
3. [finished] Did not repurpose MPIN unlock as a token-reset mechanism because the backend MPIN verify endpoint depends on an authenticated access token.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/data/auth_local_store.dart — Added explicit expiry helpers to the persisted session model so the auth layer can decide when to refresh before a token actually expires. | Notes: Added isExpiredAt and isExpiringSoon logic plus an expiresAt getter.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — Centralized proactive session refresh, retry scheduling, and timer cleanup so token lifecycle is handled in one place. | Notes: Refresh is now scheduled after restore/login and transient failures are retried instead of hard-clearing immediately.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — App resume flow now refreshes the session before resolving MPIN/security state, reducing surprise expirations after backgrounding. | Notes: This keeps resume behavior aligned with the new auth-session lifecycle.
4. [created] frontend/test/features/auth/data/auth_local_store_test.dart — Added coverage for the new expiry and proactive-refresh window helpers to protect the auth-session timing logic. | Notes: Tests cover absolute expiry and the 5-minute refresh buffer.

content:
Summary: Reviewed the name-field cleanup request and identified the exact frontend/backend boundaries. The app currently still exposes a separate second-name field in the registration and profile edit flows, while the backend profile DTOs and merge logic still accept and persist secondName. The received FHIR patient example with given=['Michael Christian','Aparicio'] should be interpreted in the UI as first name = Michael Christian and middle name = Aparicio, not as a second-name field. No SQL schema migration appears necessary because profiles already store given_names as a text array; the change is primarily field mapping and UI/API contract cleanup.

Decisions:
1. [ongoing] The cleanest implementation is to remove secondName from the app contract and keep only firstName, middleName, and lastName across registration, profile editing, and backend validation.
2. [finished] Do not introduce an SQL migration unless a separate backfill of legacy rows is desired; the current given_names array can continue to hold the normalized name parts.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/models/auth_api_models.dart — Defines the registration and profile draft contracts that currently include secondName and compose givenNames. | Notes: Needs a constructor and getter shape update.

5. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Profile edit form hydrates and saves secondName and middleName separately. | Notes: Hydration and save mapping need to collapse to first/middle/last.
6. [inspected] backend/src/auth/dto/profile-name.dto.ts — Base DTO still validates secondName and controls the API contract for registration/profile name updates. | Notes: Removing the property here propagates to both registration and profile DTOs.
7. [inspected] backend/src/auth/dto/update-profile.dto.ts — Manual profile update DTO still declares secondName explicitly. | Notes: Must be aligned with the simplified name contract.
8. [inspected] backend/src/auth/profile.service.ts — Profile merge logic currently builds given_names from firstName, secondName, and middleName. | Notes: This is the backend persistence boundary that must normalize to first/middle.

The app currently still exposes a separate second-name field in the registration and profile edit flows, while the backend profile DTOs and merge logic still accept and persist secondName. The received FHIR patient example with given=['Michael Christian','Aparicio'] should be interpreted in the UI as first name = Michael Christian and middle name = Aparicio, not as a second-name field. No SQL schema migration appears necessary because profiles already store given_names as a text array; the change is primarily field mapping and UI/API contract cleanup.

This resolves the receive-results patient matching failure caused by profile data living only in profiles.patient_profile. Summary: Implemented backend auth protections for signup email uniqueness and password reuse prevention on password reset. Added a dedicated password history table, repository, and service to hash and compare recent passwords, wired it into registration and password reset flows, and updated database typings and module providers. Also added targeted unit tests for the new auth behavior and verified the backend build passes.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Used a dedicated password history module rather than mixing hashing, comparison, and pruning into the registration or reset services, to keep auth responsibilities separated and testable.
2. [finished] Recorded the initial registration password in password history so future resets can block reuse of the original password as well as later passwords.
3. [finished] Made password-history pruning best-effort during inserts so the user-facing reset/registration flow is not blocked by a non-critical cleanup failure.
4. [finished] Used the existing normalized email/profile lookup path to enforce duplicate signup email blocking early in the registration flow.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/auth/auth.module.ts — Registered the new password history repository and service so Nest can inject them into auth flows.
2. [modified] backend/src/auth/registration-auth.service.ts — Added early duplicate-email rejection for signup and recorded the initial password in history after successful registration.
3. [modified] backend/src/auth/password-reset-auth.service.ts — Blocked password reuse on reset and rolled back password-history entries if the auth update failed.
4. [created] backend/src/auth/password-history.repository.ts — Introduced backend data access for recent password hashes, insertion, deletion, and pruning.
5. [created] backend/src/auth/password-history.service.ts — Added password hashing, comparison, and history pruning logic as a dedicated auth helper service.
6. [created] backend/src/auth/password-history.service.spec.ts — Added unit coverage for password reuse rejection and password-history insertion/pruning behavior.
7. [created] backend/src/auth/registration-auth.service.spec.ts — Added unit coverage for blocking registration when the email is already in use and recording the initial password history entry.
8. [created] backend/src/auth/password-reset-auth.service.spec.ts — Added unit coverage for blocking password reuse, writing password history on success, and cleaning up on update failure.
9. [modified] backend/src/supabase/database.types.ts — Added typed support for the new password_history_records table in Supabase database typings.
10. [created] sqls/011_password_history_setup.sql — Added the database migration for password history storage, trigger, index, and RLS denial policy.

Summary: Implemented a reusable top-of-screen notification system in the Flutter frontend and migrated snackbar callsites to it. The app now mounts a global notification host from WAH4PApp, notifications slide down as neutral cards, and screens use AppNotificationCenter instead of ScaffoldMessenger snackbars. I also added a focused widget test for the new host and updated the sync-records screen test harness to include the notification host and dismiss the shared timer state.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Use a shared app-root notification host rather than continuing to rely on ScaffoldMessenger snackbars, so the UX becomes a reusable top card and the trigger API is centralized.
2. [finished] Keep the notification styling neutral and card-like, avoiding purple-tinted accents in the new surface.
3. [finished] Represent notification types with explicit severity helpers (info, success, warning, error) so existing snackbar semantics map cleanly to the new component.

Summary: Planned a repo-wide migration from ScaffoldMessenger snackbars to a reusable top-of-screen notification card in the Flutter frontend. Discovery showed about 57 snackbar callsites across 21 files, mostly in auth, PHR, profile, interoperability, and appointments screens plus one dialog. The proposed design is a shared notification banner component plus a global controller/service mounted at the app root so notifications can slide down from the top and work from any screen or dialog.

Decisions:
1. [ongoing] Use a top-anchored, slide-down notification card instead of the default bottom SnackBar presentation because the user explicitly asked for a custom top notification surface.
2. [ongoing] Keep the notification surface neutral and card-like, avoiding purple-tinted accents in the new component to match the user styling preference.
3. [ongoing] Migrate snackbar callsites to a shared notification API rather than leaving ad hoc ScaffoldMessenger usage in individual screens.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart — This is the MaterialApp entrypoint and likely host for the global notification system. | Notes: Contains the existing navigator key and theme setup.
2. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/constants/app_colors.dart — Needed to understand the current palette and avoid introducing conflicting notification colors. | Notes: Current primary color is purple, but the new notification should not rely on purple tint.
3. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/constants/app_border_radii.dart — Needed to match the existing rounded card language for the notification surface. | Notes: All shared radii resolve to a 20px rounded style.

5. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/feature/app_bottom_sheet_widget.dart — Shows existing shared surface composition patterns for reusable feature-level widgets. | Notes: Useful styling reference for a new notification card/host.
6. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/ui/buttons/primary_button_widget.dart — Shows shared widget organization and styling conventions for reusable UI primitives. | Notes: Confirms the repo favors explicit reusable UI components.
7. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/ui/inputs/bottom_sheet_select_form_field.dart — Shows how shared widgets are structured and how overlays are used in the codebase. | Notes: Useful reference for building a reusable notification host or overlay behavior.

10. [inspected] frontend/pubspec.yaml — Needed to confirm available dependencies and avoid planning around a package that is not present. | Notes: No extra animation package is currently declared.

The personal information screen now refreshes on open, and the sync simulation flow refreshes after success; tests confirm the screen shows the synced name and lock state.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep receive-results vs receive-push semantics unchanged; the overwrite behavior belongs to profile refresh state in the app, not to changing the receive-results payload contract.
2. [finished] Use the backend profile endpoint as the source of truth for post-sync UI state so the cached AuthSession profile is replaced instead of merged locally.
3. [finished] Add optional refresh callback hooks to the Flutter screens to make the refresh path testable without network access while keeping production defaults on AuthSession.refreshProfileFromBackend.

Affected files:

Summary: Relaxed personal information save behavior so the frontend and backend no longer require all fields for profile updates, while sync remains gated strictly by identifier presence. The backend now accepts partial profile drafts, merges them with existing profile data, and keeps the sync-lock enforcement introduced earlier. Frontend form fields were changed to optional validators and the sync button still depends on having at least one identifier. Regression tests were updated and targeted Flutter/Jest suites passed.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Preserved the sync-lock behavior from the earlier sync hydration work, but decoupled it from normal save validation so users can save partial profile data before sync completion.
2. [finished] Used backend merge logic as the source of truth for partial profile updates so the frontend can stay simple and avoid duplicating field-preservation rules.
3. [finished] Kept sync availability strictly based on identifier presence instead of overall profile completeness, per the user's request.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/auth/dto/update-profile.dto.ts — Converted the profile update contract to optional fields so saving changes no longer requires filling every personal information field. | Notes: All profile fields now validate only when non-empty.
2. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.service.ts — Implemented partial-profile merge behavior and preserved sync-lock edit blocking. | Notes: Merges incoming drafts with existing row values instead of overwriting with empty strings.
3. [created] backend/src/auth/profile.service.spec.ts — Added regression coverage for partial save behavior and sync-locked update rejection. | Notes: New targeted unit tests for the profile service.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Removed required-field UI cues and switched the form to optional validation across all profile fields. | Notes: The widget now matches the relaxed save contract.
5. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Adjusted expectations so the sync button remains disabled without identifiers and the form no longer asserts required markers. | Notes: Also updated button style assertions to WidgetState.
6. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/domain/profile_sync_readiness.dart — Kept sync availability based only on identifier presence. | Notes: No longer depends on overall profile completeness.

Summary: Implemented sync/profile lock workflow across backend and frontend: sync prepare now triggers a real gateway Patient request, Patient resource mapping now hydrates internal profile fields and marks profile as sync-locked, profile API exposes lock state, and personal information UI now enables sync based on identifier-only readiness and disables editing when sync-locked. Updated regression tests for profile readiness and sync navigation behavior, and validated with targeted Flutter and backend Jest suites.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Used patient_profile.syncLocked as the lock source-of-truth instead of creating a new table/column, to keep the change minimal and compatible with existing profile JSON storage.
2. [finished] Kept sync hydration authoritative on backend webhook/resource-processing logic and only reflected lock/readiness behavior in Flutter UI to preserve clean responsibility boundaries.
3. [finished] Preserved existing auth-based simulate flow and dashboard return behavior while expanding prepare flow to initiate real gateway request.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.service.ts — Prepare sync now performs gateway Patient request and refactored gateway request helper supports POST payloads. | Notes: Added requestPatientSyncFromGateway and requestGatewayJson method split for GET/POST.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.service.spec.ts — Test expectations updated because prepare sync now performs two gateway calls (provider fetch + patient request). | Notes: Added second mocked fetch response for gateway request endpoint.
3. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Patient resource mapping now hydrates internal profile fields and marks profile as syncLocked. | Notes: Added identifier/address/telecom extraction helpers and lock marker assignment.

5. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.repository.ts — Profile response generation now computes isSyncLocked and exposes helper for lock-state detection. | Notes: Added isPatientProfileSyncLocked utility.
6. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.service.ts — Manual profile updates are now blocked when profile is sync-locked. | Notes: Added BadRequestException guard before upsert.
7. [modified] backend/src/auth/login-auth.service.ts — Default empty profile response now includes isSyncLocked for contract consistency. | Notes: Updated EMPTY_PATIENT_PROFILE_RESPONSE.
8. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/models/auth_api_models.dart — Frontend profile model now reads/writes isSyncLocked from API responses. | Notes: Extended UserProfileSummary constructor, fromJson, empty, and toJson.
9. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/domain/profile_sync_readiness.dart — Sync readiness now depends only on identifier presence as requested. | Notes: Removed completeness requirement from readiness state.
10. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Personal information screen now passes read-only state to form and uses identifier-centric sync guidance copy. | Notes: Uses AuthSession.profile.isSyncLocked for form locking.

content:
Summary: Fixed the sync simulation failure that surfaced as 'unable to store observation record' by making the backend observation/lab record filter text length-safe before insert. The root cause was an overlong laboratory_result_records.filter_value exceeding the database's 80-character check constraint. Added a reusable filter builder in the FHIR sync mapper so clinical record filter values are bounded, and added regression coverage for the PH Core parsers to assert filterValue length stays within 80 characters. Verified with targeted backend tests, backend build, and the sync simulation e2e route test.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the fix in the backend mapper rather than in the frontend, because the failure was caused by a database constraint violation on stored observation data.
2. [finished] Use a shared length-safe filter builder for clinical records so the sync flow is protected against future overlong filter values, not just the current observation fixture.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Added the length-safe filter builder and updated clinical record mapping so inserted filter values cannot exceed the database constraint. | Notes: This is the primary production fix for the observation storage failure.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.spec.ts — Added regression coverage asserting clinical record filter values stay within the 80-character database limit. | Notes: Covers the observation fixture path that previously failed.
3. [inspected] backend/package.json — Confirmed the available backend test and build scripts before running validation. | Notes: No file changes were needed.
4. [inspected] sqls/007_health_records_setup.sql — Confirmed the laboratory_result_records schema and its 80-character filter_value constraint to identify the root cause. | Notes: Read-only inspection only.
5. [inspected] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Verified the persistence flow that inserts parsed clinical resources into the repository. | Notes: No code change required in this file.

content:
Summary: Fixed the sync simulation auth-context problem by switching the frontend to send the authenticated Supabase access token as a Bearer Authorization header, and by making the backend simulation route resolve the current user from that token through AuthSupportService, with x-user-id only as a fallback. The request body no longer carries profileId, and the simulation still persists the PH Core bundle against the authenticated account via the existing receive-results-for-profile path. Validation passed after the change.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Chose the bearer-token path because the frontend already maintains AuthSession.accessToken and the backend already supports resolving authenticated Supabase users from access tokens.
2. [finished] Kept the profile UUID internal and avoided reintroducing a public profileId field, which keeps the request shape simpler and closer to the WAH4PC workflow.
3. [finished] Retained the x-user-id fallback only as a compatibility path, but made the bearer token the primary source of account context for simulation.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] backend/src/auth/auth.module.ts — Confirmed AuthSupportService is exported so the integration module can reuse existing Supabase-user resolution. | Notes: No code change required here.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.module.ts — Imported AuthModule so the simulation service can resolve the authenticated user from the access token. | Notes: Keeps auth resolution reusable rather than duplicating it.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Changed the simulation route to accept Authorization and optional x-user-id headers and pass them to the simulation service. | Notes: The route now supports bearer-token auth context.
4. [modified] backend/src/integration/sync-simulation.service.ts — Resolved profile ID from the bearer token using AuthSupportService, with x-user-id fallback, before persisting the simulated PH Core bundle. | Notes: This is the core runtime fix for the missing account context error.
5. [modified] backend/src/integration/dto/simulate-sync-request.dto.ts — Removed profileId from the public simulation DTO so the body only contains request data relevant to the sync action. | Notes: Prevents users from having to provide internal UUIDs.
6. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Removed the obsolete public simulation payload type that exposed profileId. | Notes: Keeps the contract aligned with the new auth-based flow.
7. [modified] backend/test/app.e2e-spec.ts — Updated the simulation e2e test to use a bearer Authorization header and mocked AuthSupportService account resolution. | Notes: Verifies the real auth-context path instead of the fallback header.
8. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/data/interoperability_api_client.dart — Changed the simulation client to send Authorization: Bearer <accessToken> and to support extra request headers in POST calls. | Notes: Matches the rest of the app’s authenticated API calls.
9. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/presentation/screens/sync_records_wizard_screen.dart — Switched the simulation action to use AuthSession.accessToken and show a clear sign-in prompt when the token is unavailable. | Notes: The button now uses the authenticated session token rather than the user ID.

The request body no longer carries profileId, and the simulation still persists the PH Core bundle against the authenticated account via the existing receive-results-for-profile path. Validation passed after the change.

content:
Summary: Adjusted the sync simulation flow so the user-facing request no longer carries a profileId in the body. The frontend now sends the authenticated account ID in an internal x-user-id header, while the backend simulation endpoint resolves the profile context from that header and persists the generated PH Core bundle through the existing receive-results-for-profile path. This preserves UUID-based internal storage without exposing profileId to the user and resolved the validation error the user was seeing.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept UUID-based profile IDs internally because the Supabase profiles table uses the auth user UUID as the primary key and related records reference that same key.
2. [finished] Chose an internal header for account context instead of a request-body field so the UI remains simpler and the UUID is not user-facing.
3. [finished] Preserved the existing identifier-based preparation flow and only changed the simulation transport, keeping the broader interoperability behavior stable.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/integration/dto/simulate-sync-request.dto.ts — Removed profileId from the public simulation request DTO so the request body no longer requires a UUID field from the user. | Notes: This is the main API-shape fix.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Changed the simulation route to read the internal x-user-id header and pass it to the simulation service. | Notes: Keeps account context internal to the transport layer.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/sync-simulation.service.ts — Updated the simulation service to require a header-derived user ID and persist the simulated bundle against that profile context. | Notes: No longer depends on a body-level profileId.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/data/interoperability_api_client.dart — Changed the simulation client to send x-user-id as a header instead of profileId in the body, and added header merging support to the POST helper. | Notes: The body now matches the user-facing request shape.
5. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/presentation/screens/sync_records_wizard_screen.dart — Updated the wizard to pass the signed-in session user ID into the simulation client and keep the sign-in guard for missing context. | Notes: The button stays hidden from UUID details.
6. [modified] backend/test/app.e2e-spec.ts — Updated the e2e simulation request to send x-user-id and removed the profileId body field. | Notes: Verifies the new transport shape.
7. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Removed the obsolete simulation payload type that exposed profileId in the public contract. | Notes: Prevents stale typing from reintroducing the field.

Summary: Implemented a new interoperability simulation flow so the Flutter sync wizard can post real PH Core example data into the backend and store it against the signed-in account/profile. The backend now exposes a simulated sync endpoint that reuses the existing prepare flow for validation, loads checked-in PH Core fixtures, and forwards a Bundle into the existing FHIR receive-results persistence path. The frontend gained a neutral secondary Simulate request button under Prepare request, plus request/result plumbing and updated review copy. Validation passed with backend e2e coverage and Flutter analysis.

3. [finished] Used a neutral outlined Flutter action button and neutral snackbar styling for the new simulation UX instead of adding a new purple accent treatment.

content:
Summary: Implemented a strict PH Core-aware FHIR inbound parsing layer for the backend webhook flow. The service now validates inbound receive-results and receive-push payloads through per-resource parser modules before mapping and persisting data, and the shared mapper was improved to handle array-shaped encounter code fields from PH Core examples. Added fixture-driven parser and service tests, and verified the backend e2e suite still passes.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept parsing/validation separate from persistence so the webhook service stays thin and parser behavior remains fixture-testable.
2. [finished] Used strict PH Core profile suffix matching instead of loose payload acceptance for supported resource families.
3. [finished] Kept the existing Supabase repository write methods and fed them normalized parser output rather than rewriting persistence logic.
4. [finished] Updated the shared encounter mapping to handle array-shaped FHIR codeable-concept fields because the PH Core encounter fixture uses that shape.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Wired inbound webhook handling through the new parser registry before persistence, keeping route handling thin and validating PH Core payloads first. | Notes: receiveResults and receivePush now parse resources before repository writes.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Improved shared mapping logic for array-shaped codeable-concept fields so PH Core encounter payloads normalize correctly. | Notes: Encounter titles now resolve from array-shaped type fields.
3. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.profile.ts — Centralized strict PH Core profile suffix rules for supported FHIR resource families. | Notes: Used by parser modules to validate declared profiles.
4. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.types.ts — Defined the discriminated union types for parsed inbound patient and clinical resources. | Notes: Keeps parser outputs explicit and typed.
5. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.utils.ts — Added shared parser boundary helpers for resource-type validation and PH Core profile detection. | Notes: Shared by all resource-specific parsers.
6. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/clinical.parser.ts — Implemented the generic clinical resource parser that validates and normalizes supported non-patient FHIR payloads. | Notes: Delegates resource-specific mapping to the existing mapper.
7. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/patient.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core patient parser boundary. | Notes: Returns the validated Patient resource for profile updates and identifier matching.
8. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/immunization.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core immunization parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
9. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/encounter.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core encounter parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.
10. [created] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/observation.parser.ts — Implemented the PH Core observation parser boundary. | Notes: Wraps the generic clinical parser.

Summary: Fixed the PowerShell FHIR seed script so it no longer fails on duplicate profile primary keys. The seed flow now treats the profile created by the auth trigger as an upsert target and also upserts patient_identifiers, making reruns idempotent after partial attempts. The script still seeds PH Core example bundles into /fhir/receive-results for immunization, consultation history, medical history, and laboratory results.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Treat the auth trigger-created profile as authoritative and update it via upsert rather than trying to insert a second profile row.
2. [finished] Keep the seed script idempotent so it can be rerun safely after partial failures without manual cleanup.

Affected files:
1. [modified] scripts/seed-fhir-sync.ps1 — Changed the seed flow to upsert profile and patient identifier rows instead of inserting them directly. | Notes: This is the direct fix for the duplicate key error.
2. [inspected] sqls/001_auth_setup.sql — Confirmed the auth.users trigger that auto-creates or updates profiles, explaining the duplicate key failure. | Notes: No code changes were required.
3. [inspected] backend/src/auth/profile.repository.ts — Verified the application uses profile upsert semantics already, which informed the seed script fix. | Notes: No code changes were required.

content:
Summary: Implemented Phase 2 of the interoperability rollout across backend and Flutter. The backend now exposes a WAH4PC-backed interoperability proxy for provider discovery and sync-request preparation, with env validation and e2e coverage. The Flutter app now launches a dedicated sync wizard from the Personal Information screen, uses bottom-sheet selection for identifier/provider choice, and reuses the existing profile readiness rules to gate entry. Tests for the new interoperability models, wizard flow, and profile-to-wizard navigation all pass.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep backend gateway communication isolated in a dedicated integration module rather than calling wah4pc directly from Flutter, so provider discovery and request prep stay server-owned.
2. [finished] Keep the sync wizard as a route-based multi-step screen rather than collapsing it into a modal, because the flow needs visible step state and room for provider/error handling.
3. [finished] Use a generic secure-storage mock in flutter_test_config.dart instead of per-test stubbing, so existing and future widget tests can call AuthSession.clear() safely.

Summary: Implemented Phase 1 of the interoperability rollout on the Flutter frontend by adding a reusable profile sync-readiness helper, gating the Personal Information screen sync action behind profile completeness plus at least one identifier, and relaxing identifier inputs so PhilHealth ID and PhilSys ID are optional individually while sync requires one of them. Added tests for the readiness helper and updated the Personal Information screen test to cover the locked state and disabled sync button. The phase 1 Kanban card was moved from backlog to in-progress and then to done after validation.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the gateway sync implementation out of scope for Phase 1 and only prepare the profile/readiness layer now, so the later gateway work can build on a stable profile gate.
2. [finished] Preserve both identifier fields in the data model for backward compatibility and future support, but relax the UI so sync only needs one of them instead of requiring both.
3. [finished] Use a small domain helper for readiness logic instead of embedding the rules directly in the screen, to keep presentation and sync rules separated.

Affected files:
1. [created] frontend/lib/features/profile/domain/profile_sync_readiness.dart — Contains the reusable readiness rules used to decide whether sync records can start and to format missing requirements for the UI. | Notes: New pure helper for phase 1 sync gating.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Implements the visible sync readiness card and disables or enables the sync records action based on the profile state. | Notes: Also updates the sync placeholder message and wires the new helper into the screen.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Relaxes identifier inputs and updates the identifiers section copy so the profile can be saved without forcing both identifier fields. | Notes: PhilHealth ID and PhilSys ID are optional now.
4. [created] frontend/test/features/profile/domain/profile_sync_readiness_test.dart — Adds unit coverage for the sync readiness helper, including identifier gating and readable missing-field formatting. | Notes: New helper tests.
5. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Updates the widget expectations to match the new sync readiness card and optional identifier fields. | Notes: Also checks the sync button is disabled when the profile is incomplete.

Summary: Implemented the appointments structure update in the frontend. The booking flow now ends with a dedicated review screen instead of a confirmation modal, and the appointments/health-records hubs now expose consultation history as a first-class destination. The consultation history screen reuses the existing health-records backend-backed flow and is relabeled for clearer UX.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep onsite consultation and teleconsultation as thin wrappers around the shared booking screen so the flow stays DRY and mode-driven.
2. [finished] Use the existing health-records consultations backend path for consultation history instead of creating a separate duplicate history API.
3. [finished] Use a dedicated full-screen review step for confirmation because it is more readable and easier to review than a modal on mobile.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/appointments/presentation/models/appointment_booking_models.dart — Added booking summary data and updated mode copy to support the dedicated review screen and clearer step guidance. | Notes: Also introduced review-title copy for the review screen header.
2. [modified] frontend/lib/features/appointments/presentation/screens/appointment_booking_screen.dart — Changed the final step to push a dedicated review screen, removed the confirmation modal, and kept the shared booking flow intact. | Notes: The screen remains the shared shell for onsite and teleconsultation flows.
3. [created] frontend/lib/features/appointments/presentation/screens/appointment_review_screen.dart — Added the dedicated full-screen confirmation/review step for appointment bookings. | Notes: Returns a boolean result back to the booking flow on confirmation.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/dashboard/presentation/screens/appointments_screen.dart — Added consultation history as a hub action so the appointments page exposes booking and history paths. | Notes: Routes the new action to the existing consultation history backend-backed screen.
5. [modified] frontend/lib/features/dashboard/presentation/screens/health_records_screen.dart — Renamed the consultations tile to consultation history for clearer terminology in the health-records hub. | Notes: Still uses the same medical consultations route constant.
6. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_consultations_screen.dart — Relabeled the consultations screen as consultation history and updated its help copy to match the new UX. | Notes: Still uses HealthRecordSection.consultations, so it remains backend-backed.
